teamObjective.explanation: With only low non-trump clubs, I expect this opening lead to be beaten. The goal is to lose as cheaply as possible while disposing of my weakest card and preserving stronger low cards for later tricks.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) has 3 cards and has made no public plays, so they could hold a trump or high non-trump that might beat opponents on this trick. I cannot assume partner strength, only that they share the team objective.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards and is near finishing, so opponents are likely trying to win the trick and maintain initiative to let player 1 exit. They likely have cards that beat a low club, possibly including trumps.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Lead the 3 of clubs because it is the lowest, cheapest card. It removes my weakest liability, gives away minimal strength, and keeps the 4, 5, and 6 available for future plays where they might be more useful.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is still a low non-trump card with little chance to win the trick; leading it wastes a slightly better card that could be useful later.
The 5 is higher than the 3 and 4 but still not high enough to be likely to win; playing it now sacrifices mid-strength without compensating benefit.
The 6 is my highest club, but as a non-trump below the trump rank it is very likely to lose to any trump or higher non-trump. Dumping it first weakens my remaining hand unnecessarily.

risks: Opponent player 1 may win the trick and use the initiative to finish their remaining 2 cards.
Partner may not have a high enough card to beat opponents if they choose to play.
All my cards are low non-trump, so I have limited ability to regain control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the 3 minimizes the value lost on this trick, reduces my hand size, and leaves the 6 as my best remaining low card for a later opportunity or to cover a slightly higher lead.
